data.title: Snap: Losing Relevance, But Too Cheap To Ignore
data.description:
It may be time to acknowledge that Snap Inc. (NYSE:SNAP) is no longer the high-growth story it once was and may never return to that status. The company is posting weaker fundamentals than larger peers, a trend that may suggest a continual weakening of the social network. I expect an activist or outright takeover to be a lingering potential outcome for the stock unless management decides to bring focus to driving profit margins higher. The stock trades very cheaply but investors may be stuck in a no-man’s zone given the lack of GAAP profitability. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

SNAP Stock Price

I last covered SNAP in July, where I rated the stock a buy because of the margin expansion opportunity. The stock has underperformed the broader market by around 17% since then.

SNAP is not seeing metrics trend in the right direction but the stock is too cheap to give up on just yet.

SNAP Stock Key Metrics

The social media company, which in my own words was the “TikTok of the past,” has struggled to adapt in recent years. Whereas Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) appears to have successfully implemented AI into its algorithms to fend off the TikTok threat, SNAP has not had the same success. The most recent quarter saw average daily active users (‘DAUs’) come in at 469 million, jumping 9% YoY and slightly outperforming guidance for 468 million. But the company saw its most important (and lucrative) user base in North America see sequential declines yet again to 98 million.

The company saw average revenue per user (‘ARPU’) stagnate for the first time since 2023, but that was mainly due to the rise in international users. The company saw North America ARPU jump 9% YoY, helping to offset the weakness in DAUs.

The company saw overall revenues jump 9% YoY to $1.345 billion. I note that “other revenue” jumped 64% YoY to $171 million (composed primarily of the Snapchat+ subscription service) and made up the majority of that gain.

The company was unable to drive margin expansion, with adjusted EBITDA margins retreating by 100 bps.

The company ended the quarter with $2.9 billion in cash versus $3.5 billion in debt, representing the only net debt balance sheet among the larger publicly traded social media peers.

Looking ahead, management guided for the third quarter to see revenue fall between $1.475 billion to $1.505 billion, representing 9.6% YoY growth, and for adjusted EBITDA to be between $110 million and $135 million. These results, as well as the guidance, significantly trailed those of the larger META. In the past, a key bullish thesis was that SNAP was a “smaller META” and would see accelerated growth due to the supposed longer growth runway. That thesis has been thrown out the door.

On the conference call, management noted that advertising revenue grew at just a 1% pace in April and ended up around “3% to 4%” by the end of the quarter. In my view, at this stage in the game, advertising revenue is a more coveted revenue stream as it signals higher relevance. To this end, management noted that while North American users have struggled to grow, they have seen “North America unique Snap senders” show 2% YoY growth and video calling see 30% YoY growth. While I am bullish on the stock, I am far less optimistic regarding these data points.

Is SNAP Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, SNAP traded hands at just over 2x sales. That represented a steep discount to the 6x multiple at Pinterest, Inc. (PINS), the 10x multiple at Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and the 26x multiple at Reddit, Inc. (RDDT). Consensus estimates look too aggressive as they are factoring in acceleration in top-line growth. While I could understand how the small revenue base might inspire confidence upon an eventual acceleration, the fact that advertising revenue growth has been so modest despite strong results at peers suggests that growth rates are more likely to head lower, perhaps to the mid-single-digits.

Even so, I see value here. I expect the company to generate at least 25% net margins over the long term, which would be around half the margins seen at META’s advertising businesses. That places the current valuation at just over 8x long-term earnings. The path from here to there, unfortunately, has become more cloudy, though if the company can maintain some level of top-line growth, then eventual cost discipline may lead to operating leverage. At this point, however, I am of the view that a possible takeover might be an important catalyst. I would never invest solely on takeover potential, but the stock’s low valuation plus lack of consistency with regards to profitability suggests that some drastic action might be needed here. Outside of that, I view the main catalyst in the near to medium term to be an eventual focus on cost discipline and margin expansion. I could see the stock eventually re-rating around 50% higher to a 3x sales multiple, which would equate to a 12x long-term earnings multiple. The main challenge is that management has not yet shown any indication that they intend to focus on profitability.

SNAP Stock Key Risks

It is possible that SNAP is really simply getting disrupted. Social networks work well when the network is vibrant but may prove to have negative feedback loops in the opposing case. It is possible that SNAP can never drive profit margins higher. Perhaps operating leverage proves difficult if growth turns negative. This thesis appears to have an increasingly greater reliance on a potential takeover offer, which might never come to fruition.

SNAP Stock Conclusion

A cheap valuation does not always mean a buy rating. But at SNAP, that valuation is very cheap, and the company maintains a respectable balance sheet and is arguably not that far from achieving a GAAP breakeven profile. The stock looks cheap enough to hold and wait for management to eventually bring a focus on driving margin expansion. That said, investors might prefer the stock of META as I have in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my buy rating for SNAP stock.

data.title: Roche's 89bio Buyout: Building A Combo-Ready Metabolic Franchise Beyond GLP-1 Wars
data.description:
Introduction

Roche Holding AG (OTCQX:RHHBY) is doubling down on its war against cardiometabolic diseases after announcing its intent to acquire 89bio, Inc. (NASDAQ:ETNB) for pegozafermin, which is described in the NEJM as a “long-acting glycopegylated (pegylated with the use of site-specific glycosyltransferases) fibroblast growth factor 21 (FGF21) analogue.”

This latest deal follows its acquisition of Carmot in December 2023 for its GLP-1/GIP assets (I wrote an article following that purchase), like CT-388, and a $5.3 billion collaboration with Zealand Pharma for amylin (pentrelintide). Interestingly, Roche is studying 388 in combination with pentrelintide.

In the article that follows, I propose that pegozafermin effectively diversifies the axis and positions Roche for future leadership in the massive cardiometabolic market (including indications like MASH and obesity), meriting a more Bullish stance.

The Shifting Landscape in MASH and Obesity

This is not my first rodeo with MASH nor obesity drug development. This is a space I’ve been following closely for years. So, let’s begin by adding some context.

The FDA approval of Madrigal’s (MDGL) Rezdiffra’s (a thyroid beta agonist) was a watershed moment for MASH, as it represented the first and only drug indicated for MASH. Despite entering the market in mid-2024, Rezdiffra is already nearing $1 billion in annualized revenues ($212.8 million in Q2 alone). Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) Wegovy, a GLP-1 agonist, expanded its obesity label to receive approval in MASH (noncirrhotic with F2-F3 fibrosis) in August. However, Wegovy is not expected to “cannibalize” Rezdiffra. Rather, the prospects of combination therapy in MASH are increasingly real, bearing in mind that this is still a very young market.

Make no mistake about it. GLP-1s, like Wegovy, are being positioned (at least so far in treatment recommendations I pay attention to) as first-line for many patients (e.g., those who are obese/diabetic). For the patients who don’t have access to Wegovy or who can’t tolerate it or who have an insufficient response, Rezdiffra is a solid option.

We are seeing real-world evidence that Rezdiffra and Wegovy are being utilized at the same time. In fact, Madrigal’s management explicitly details this on occasion (“25% are with combo therapy. So they are on GLP-1 for another indication”).

Of course, there are several other drugs in late-stage development in MASH. Many are sporting different MOAs. We also have to think about this in the context of different markets. Obesity is capacity- and rebate-intensive. On the other hand, MASH and severe hypertriglyceridemia (SHTG) live in specialty budgets with clearer medical-necessity hooks. A MASH/SHTG label – if won – is a nearer-term and a cleaner reimbursement story than broad obesity. Just as is the case in obesity drug development, consensus is shifting towards combination in MASH. We want to see fibrosis and lipid signals that are additive to incretins (GLP-1, GIP, glucagon) or THY-β.

Pegozafermin is making good progress in MASH following histology-positive Phase 2b data (ENLIVEN). Delivered via subcutaneous injection on a weekly or bi-weekly basis, pegozafermin has already demonstrated fibrosis improvement and MASH resolution at 24 weeks. The Phase 3 study (ENLIGHTEN-Cirrhosis), initiated in mid-2024, will evaluate pegozafermin over 24 months. The original intention was that “regression of fibrosis by histology at month 24” could be sufficient for accelerated approval. As is usual in MASH development, full approval hinges on future clinical outcomes like mortality and liver failure.

Simultaneously, pegozafermin is being studied in a Phase 3 trial (ENTRUST) for SHTG with a focus on triglyceride lowering. Triglyceride lowering is a surrogate with established clinical relevance (pancreatitis risk). So, this gives Roche some optionality.

Back to MASH, focus is shifting on high-fibrosis subsets (which drive events and costs, supporting specialty pricing), especially as GLP-1s have reported mixed efficacy on liver fibrosis. Within the FGF21 class, Akero’s (AKRO) efruxifermin (dosed weekly), too, achieved positive Phase 2 results in F2/F3 MASH patients. So, this really helps de-risk the class in F2/F3. Notably, pegozafermin’s efficacy held up to every two-week dosing, which may be a key differentiator from efruxifermin. However, efruxifermin failed in F4 (compensated) MASH. Pegozafermin’s Phase 3 has an F4/cirrhosis arm. So, while efruxifermin is squarely a MASH-first play, pegozafermin has a better histology + lipid story with QW/Q2W dosing and a registrational SHTG path.

Asset Mechanism Lead indication / stage Key efficacy signals Differentiators / watch-outs
Pegozafermin (89bio/Roche) Long-acting FGF21 analog MASH Ph3 (ENLIGHTEN); SHTG Ph3 (ENTRUST) Histology: fibrosis↓ & MASH resolution at 24w; TG↓, non-HDL↓ QW/Q2W dosing; dual path (MASH + SHTG). Ph3 histology risk; class durability.
Efruxifermin (Akero) Fc-fusion FGF21 MASH Ph3 (post-P2b) F2–F3: fibrosis improvement at 24w; F4 36w primary missed (longer-term mixed) Strong pre-cirrhosis data; F4 uncertainty; weekly only.
Resmetirom (Madrigal) THR-β agonist Approved: non-cirrhotic NASH (F2–F3) Histology-based approval; LDL-C↓, atherogenic lipids↓ First-mover; outcomes confirmatory ongoing; combo backbone potential.
GLP-1s (e.g., Wegovy) GLP-1RA (±GIP, ±glucagon) Obesity approved; MASH label expansion (select) Weight↓, liver fat↓; fibrosis effects variable Supply/pricing constraints; powerful combo partner; payer dynamics heavy.

In fact, Roche’s deal (totaling $3.5 billion → $2.4 billion at closing) shields it from F4 upsets (e.g., $2 CVR upon the first commercial sale in F4 MASH), given past results for efruxifermin. The deal also implies that pegozafermin could very well become a multi-billion dollar drug ($1.50 CVR if annual net sales are >$3 billion).

In pharmaceuticals, IP is king. Roche’s Carmot (incretins) + Zealand (amylin) + 89bio (FGF21) stack the company’s obesity-adjacent CVRM (cardio-vascular, renal, and metabolic diseases) and combinations could reasonably extend lifecycle. There are also some broader implications associated with Roche’s cardiometabolic strategy. It is effectively avoiding direct GLP-1 “me-too” obesity escalation and, instead, capturing adjacency where payers already reimburse (e.g., lipids, MASH) and avoiding “squaring-up” with established pharmaceutical behemoths like Eli Lilly (LLY) (with Zepbound) and Novo Nordisk (Wegovy).

Quick Business Overview

Taking a step back, Roche’s top products (see 1H results) include Ocrevus (multiple sclerosis), Vabysmo (ophthalmology), Xolair (immunology), and Phesgo (HER2). Like many big pharma, Roche is having to deal with its legacy assets losing exclusivity (such as Avastin, Herceptin, and Lucentis. Notably, an Xolair biosimilar was recently approved. Ocrevus baseline COM protection starts rolling off ~2029. So, Roche has several billion in “revenue at risk” that necessitate replacement in the coming years. With that said, this has (and will) always be the case in pharmaceuticals, and Roche hasn't been around for 100+ years for no good reason.

Meanwhile, RHHBY is growing its revenues at 4.8% (YoY versus 6.71% sector median) and is highly profitable (net income margin of 14.85%). Within this context, its forward-looking P/E ratio of 16.15 (versus 24.78 sector median) appears to be pricing in some challenges ahead. However, current analyst estimates suggest that Roche will be able to maintain low-single-digit growth in the coming years. Roche has $15.16 billion in cash at its disposal with $43.65 billion in total debt. I suspect that a major (Phase 3) cardiometabolic win could shift growth expectations into the mid-single-digits, supporting a valuation re-rating.

The Bottom Line

To wrap up, Roche is quietly positioning itself as a leader in the cardiometabolic market without having to be “face-to-face” with obesity leaders like Novo and Eli Lilly. Its most recent acquisition of 89bio and pegozafermin is a relatively small bet that serves to diversify its approach in a way that is truly unique. Meanwhile, the current climate (e.g., LOEs) demands later-stage assets to effectively overcome its revenue at risk, and I think Roche is taking steps in the right direction on this matter, meriting another Buy rating.

data.title: If The U.S. Economy Is So Good...
data.description:
Earlier this week, August retail sales rose a robust 0.6% on a month-over-month basis. This was higher than expected and follows a revised 0.6% rise in retail sales in July and an even stronger 0.9% gain in June. The latest Atlanta FedNow forecast for Q3 GDP growth came out Wednesday. It currently predicts 3.4% GDP growth in the third quarter.

This follows a revised 3.3% GDP growth rate in the second quarter of this year. On the surface, the U.S. economy appears to be in good shape. However, if one digs just a little deeper, there are numerous trends that should not be happening in a strong economy. Here are several that are on my radar.

If the economy is so strong, why is tourism falling sharply in Las Vegas? Sin City has been a solid canary in the coalmine for alerting investors historically that the overall economy is turning down. Tourist visits in May dropped by 6.5% compared to the same month in 2024 and were down 5.1% in April. Reid International Airport saw passenger count drop by nearly 4% in May as well. In June, tourist visits dropped a whopping 11.3% from the same month a year ago. For the first half of 2025, tourist traffic fell by 7.3%.

If the economy is so strong, why are student loan delinquencies surging into the low teens? And this follows nearly four years of taxpayer-financed payment forbearance, that should, in theory, have helped loan holders get their financial house in order. Instead, nearly 6 million student loan holders out of approximately 42 million are currently at least 90 days behind in their payments.

If the economy is so strong, why is the job market faltering? With the latest August BLS report showing just 22,000 positions being created during the month and the original estimates for June and July being sharply revised lower, the U.S. economy on average created just less than 30,000 jobs per month from June through August. And with AI capabilities improving by the month, should one think corporations will be in a hiring mood in 2026?

If the economy is so strong, why are auto loan delinquencies surging with auto loan debt now over $1.65 trillion? Subprime auto delinquencies are above their levels during the peak of the Great Financial Crisis. Of course, with the average new vehicle price running just under $50,000 and nearly one of five new auto loans running seven years and 20% of new auto loans running at $1,000 a month or more, is it any wonder a larger number of American households are behind on their vehicle loans?

If the economy is so strong, why are consumer sentiment readings in the dumpster? And as I recall, the consumer is nearly 70% of U.S. economic activity.

If the economy is so strong, why is the housing market tanking in so many regions of the country? Average housing prices have already dropped 10% to 25% from their peaks in metro areas such as Austin, Oakland, Denver, New Orleans, D.C., Cape Coral, Birmingham, San Francisco, and Phoenix. Annual existing home sales from 2023 to 2025 have run at their lowest levels since 1995, when the U.S. population was significantly smaller. Average new home prices were at a discount to average existing home prices in June and July. That is the first time this has happened since records began being kept.

If the economy is so strong, why are personal and business bankruptcies growing at a concerning pace?

It is becoming apparent to me the U.S. economy is being supported by very few pillars right now. The first is the federal government running a fiscal deficit of 6%-7% of GDP in recent years. With interest needed to service an over $37 trillion obligation, obviously this is unsustainable at some point.

Then we have huge investments in building AI data centers being made by tech giants like Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOG). Will they pay off in the years ahead and produce large amounts of return on investment, or will some of these investments turn out like the tens of billions Meta Platforms (META) sunk into building out the Metaverse?

Finally, one other thing driving the economy is the small slice of the American population who saw price appreciation in their assets (stocks, real estate, etc...) outpace the rising cost of living over the past five years. The top 10% of income earners (roughly $250,000 of annual income or higher) are now responsible for nearly half of all consumer spending.

They also own 87% of all equity value. If housing values continue to decline and/or stocks start to falter, this key source of consumer spending could weaken.

In summary, the economy is weaker than being portrayed on many fronts. The market is trading at/near all-time highs and selling at extreme levels using many traditional valuation metrics. A prudent investor has to be increasingly skeptical that the few, key economic engines still firing can continue to support the economy and the markets at current levels. Therefore, my portfolio continues to be positioned quite conservatively (roughly one-quarter in short-term treasuries, three-quarters in covered call holdings).

data.title: Pzena U.S. Small Cap Focused Value Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Markets were volatile during the quarter, as “Liberation Day” announcements led to uncertainty around tariffs, domestic demand, and a potential recession.

As President Trump softened his stance throughout the quarter, stocks rebounded and more than offset the initial weakness. Small-cap stocks underperformed large-cap stocks, and value stocks underperformed growth. Our portfolio underperformed its benchmark during the period.

The basic materials, consumer staples, and health care sectors detracted from performance. The largest individual detractors were all businesses negatively impacted by potential tariffs and fears that higher prices could impact spending. Consumer products company Helen of Troy (HELE) manufactures most of its products in Asia. Their sourcing footprint is in line with competitors, so it is unlikely that they will have a cost disadvantage, but higher pricing could negatively impact demand for their discretionary products. Similarly, Spectrum Brands (SPB), a consumer products company with a diverse product portfolio, has a personal appliance business that sources from Asia. X-ray tube manufacturer Varex Imaging (VREX) sells products produced in the U.S. into China, so China’s new reciprocal tariffs have impacted their competitiveness versus European suppliers.

The top contributing sectors this quarter were industrials, financials, and consumer discretionary. Automotive seat manufacturer Adient (ADNT) declined during the first quarter after initial automotive tariffs were announced, but shares have more than rebounded as tariffs were reduced. Specialty vehicle manufacturer Shyft Group (SHYF) also rebounded, as tariffs have less of an impact on its portfolio of businesses, and the company merged with German competitor Aebi Schmidt (AEBI). Snowplow manufacturer Douglas Dynamics (PLOW) was supported by improved snowfall trends and anticipation that the company can weather tariff risks due to its U.S.-focused supply chain.

We initiated a position in medical device company Teleflex (TFX). Shares underperformed due to the disappointing performance of a urology product line and concerns around management’s plan to spin off underperforming businesses in an abrupt change of strategy. We believe this masks a strong core business with leading positions in defensible, high-margin niche businesses. We added to staffer Robert Half and furniture retailer Haverty on weakness. We exited animal pharmaceutical company Phibro Animal Health (PAHC) and automotive axle manufacturer Dana on strength. We also exited door and window manufacturer JELD-WEN (JELD), as the range of outcomes widened dramatically due to the courts forcing it to divest a business at a highly depressed valuation. We shifted the position into cabinet manufacturers MasterBrand (MBC) and American Woodmark (AMWD). We also trimmed tobacco leaf merchant Universal on strength.

Markets have been volatile, and small-cap stocks remain particularly depressed, as government actions and macro uncertainty have led to fear. This is not abnormal for periods such as these, but we continue to feel positive about our portfolio, which is positioned toward more economically sensitive and cyclical names, as valuations remain quite attractive.

data.title: Pzena U.S. Focused Value Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Markets were volatile throughout the second quarter, as “Liberation Day” tariff announcements created uncertainty around domestic demand, prompting recession concerns. As the Trump administration moderated its stance on trade over the course of the quarter, equities rebounded to above pre-Liberation Day levels. The broad U.S. market ended the period up double digits, effectively erasing its year-to-date performance gap with the value index, and our portfolio slightly underperformed its value benchmark.

Financials and consumer discretionary were the outsized positive performers in the quarter, while technology also contributed to absolute performance. Discount retailer Dollar General (DG) appreciated meaningfully, as same- store sales improved and gross margins expanded, driven by increased traffic from middle-income consumers trading down to the discount channel, along with improved inventory control measures and a labor boost that helped reduce theft. The company’s limited exposure to imports also positions it favorably in a tariff-sensitive environment. Financials’ strong performance was supported by benign credit losses, favorable stress test outcomes, a more accommodative regulatory backdrop, and robust capital markets activity. Citigroup (C) was among our top contributors in the quarter, delivering low single-digit revenue growth and declining expenses, resulting in improved returns on capital. Capital One (COF) was another key contributor, with continued solid execution and the successful closing of its Discover acquisition—a transaction the market views as offering significant cost synergies.

Health care was the weakest-performing sector, followed by industrials and energy. Staffing company Robert Half (RHI) detracted, as persistent macro weakness has negatively impacted demand for temporary labor. The chemical industry remained under pressure, with Dow (DOW) continuing to struggle, as the company further delayed its Alberta capex plans while announcing additional facility closures in Europe. Lastly, merchant acquirer Global Payments (GPN) announced a transformational acquisition of Worldpay during the quarter. The deal surprised investors, given management’s prior emphasis on returning capital to shareholders in lieu of M&A. After meeting with the company and conducting extensive due diligence, we gained conviction in the strategic rationale for the transaction, as the combined entity will process $4 trillion in annual payment volumes and offer Global Payments the opportunity to expand distribution of its Genius point- of-sale product.

During the quarter, we initiated a position in Oshkosh (OSK), a leading manufacturer of aerial work platforms (AWP), vocational vehicles, wheeled military vehicles, and post office vehicles. The company is materially underearning in its AWP and defense segments, but should benefit as the AWP replacement cycle materializes and as its postal delivery truck contract ramps up. We also added to Robert Half (RHI), Skyworks (SWKS), and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), and trimmed Wells Fargo (WFC), Equitable (EQH), and TE Connectivity (TEL), all on strength.

Although markets remain volatile amid ongoing government actions and macroeconomic uncertainty, this environment is not unusual for such periods. We remain constructive on the portfolio, where valuations continue to appear attractive across a broad range of sectors.

Capital One (COF) was another key contributor, with continued solid execution and the successful closing of its Discover acquisition—a transaction the market views as offering significant cost synergies.

Health care, pressured by concerns around drug pricing, was the weakest-performing sector, followed by energy and consumer staples. Drugmaker Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) was the largest individual detractor due to disappointing results from an antipsychotic drug trial and the acquisition of a Chinese drug compound at a higher-than-expected price. In what was a notable development, merchant acquirer Global Payments (GPN) announced a transformational acquisition of Worldpay during the quarter. The deal surprised investors, given management’s prior emphasis on returning capital to shareholders in lieu of M&A. After meeting with the company and conducting extensive due diligence, we gained conviction in the strategic rationale for the transaction, as the combined entity will process $4 trillion in annual payment volumes and offer Global Payments the opportunity to expand distribution of its Genius point-of-sale product. The chemical industry remained under pressure, with Dow (DOW) continuing to struggle, as the company further delayed its Alberta capex plans while announcing additional facility closures in Europe.

In addition to increasing our position in Global Payments, we added to Corebridge (CRBG) (formerly AIG’s life & retirement unit) and Halliburton (oil services). We trimmed positions in Charter Communications (CHTR), Dollar General, and CVS (CVS), all on strength.

Although markets remain volatile amid ongoing government actions and macroeconomic uncertainty, this environment is not unusual for such periods.

We remain constructive on the portfolio, where valuations continue to appear attractive across a broad range of sectors.

data.title: Dream Industrial REIT: Miss This Monthly Dividend Payer And Regret It Later
data.description:
A flight to safety might occur sooner than you think

"Growth at a reasonable price" investors that seek for a combination of value and growth don't have to look any further. As I've shown in my article on another REIT that was published yesterday, the Shiller P/E shows us that running from the big indices makes a ton of sense in the current environment. Even OpenAI's CEO called AI important but also an overhyped bubble. So even tech CEO's dare to mention the fact that investing in their own [tech] space should be flagged as a risky endeavor right now. Some food for thought, maybe?

I'm currently invested in one micro-cap technology company, but 98% of our entire investment portfolio is allocated to REITs. But before people scream at me that I am overly concentrated, let me tell you something: REITs have a pretty predictable revenue stream and even thought they are susceptible to changes in the interest rate set by the Central Banks, analysts that do their homework can pretty accurately analyze these companies and come up with a narrow valuation (when taking a long-term approach). REITs have been underperforming in recent years, but if you look at 1972 - 2024, REITs have outperformed the S&P 500 by an average of 4.6 percentage points per year. When you are investing right now, it's hard to believe that REITs outperformed for many years. But they did and that's why I am bullish on REITs going forward. Even more so on a relative basis.

I don't know a lot about technology companies. I can read the headline numbers just like you can, but making bold predictions about a firm's competitive advantage? Susceptibility to innovative disruptions? Most of the people that I hear bragging about their "awesome returns on their tech-investments" actually scare me. Market euphoria, in general, scares me.

Just let me show you one graph that - to me - illustrates risk. Take your time before continuing to read.

The answer is: yes. You are looking at the data correctly. When COVID-19 hit, the Nasdaq (for good reasons) was valued higher than REITs, by a decent margin. But than came the "work from home" movement, followed by big rate hikes and - as we all know by now - the AI-boom. The Nasdaq (and because of overexposure to tech, the S&P 500 as well) surged to all-time highs. Outperforming the Vanguard REIT index by a huge margin.

As I said in my article just yesterday: I won't follow the herd. Sure, the big indices could keep climbing higher and higher. But at these valuations (Palantir (PLTR) at a 561 P/E ratio and Tesla (TSLA) at a 256 P/E ratio), any headwind in earnings could crater these stocks (and with it, the entire market).

As shown below, many Seeking Alpha analysts, thankfully, are aware that a bubble is forming. The question is not will it pop. The real question is: will you sell your shares before the inevitable mean reversion happens? Many investors think they will. That's the problem time and time again.

Growth at an unreasonably low price

Today, I will take you with me over one of my new REIT picks that I will gladly buy into when my next paycheck hits my bank account. For the first time, we won't be talking about another U.S. REIT today. I will be looking at the Canadian market, more specifically Dream Industrial Real Estate Investment Trust (OTC:DREUF). A REIT that even lagged the already lagging Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) by around 42 percentage point in the past 10+ years. As I will show you in a bit, I believe that this REIT is unreasonable low priced and has the potential to provide investors with value as well as growth when bought at today's price.

Before we continue, let's take a quick look at the (peer) data that Seeking Alpha provides us. First of all, I have excluded Innovative Industrial Properties Trust (IIPR). I wrote about this REIT back in January, but it's a Cannabis-focused REIT in an industry that is clouded by regulatory headwinds, tenant defaults and unsustainable high rent escalators. It is more of a deep-value play at the current price, so not a real GARP contender. A fellow author warned the market time and time again about Industrial Logistics Properties Trust (ILPT) because of its dangerously laddered debt that will lead to refinancing issues. But even worse, a history of being externally managed led to shareholder destruction (for example by doing an equity issuance at depressed prices). So we can skip that REIT as well, even though it seems a good pick at the surface (and according to certain analysts).

We are left with three REITs that I haven't covered yet: LXP Industrial Trust (LXP), Americold Realty Trust (COLD) and Plymouth Industrial REIT (PLYM). The last one seems fairly valued at a pretty low starting (dividend) yield, and COLD is more of a specialized REIT aimed at cold storage / temperature-controlled warehouses. Technically "Industrial," but more of a niche subsector of this REIT category.

This leaves us with LXP. This stock doesn't really show great quant statistics when looking at the information below. In the past three years, it has lagged DREUF in total returns by quite a margin.

In the following chapters, I will try to share with you my realistic and bullish outlook on Dream Industrial. DREUF looks to be doing well compared to peers, especially considered that it trades at a pretty low valuation compared to its own historic trading range. This is a specific valuation metric that I prefer over a peer analysis, since this data actually gives us the valuation that Mr. Market historically gave to Dream Industrial specifically.

Sometimes I use a REIT's historical AFFO multiple, but that works best with triple net REITs. These REITs have leases in which tenants are responsible for most of the taxes, insurance and maintenance costs. Narrowing the gap between Funds From Operations (FFO) and Adjusted Funds From Operations, or AFFO. Since DREUF doesn't directly report AFFO numbers, I will use its own historic P/FFO ratio.

What's interesting is that we are talking about a quite volatile stock. At the peak of 2021, everyone bought Industrial REITs because of the work from home trend and the fact that we couldn't go outside so we ordered a lot of goods and services. Great for REITs that focus on warehouses. DREUF traded at a 22x P/FFO multiple. Back in 2015, it traded as low as 6.4x. As of today, the average (normalized) P/FFO multiple sits at 12.3x. In line with the current P/FFO multiple. More on this in a bit. With a Beta of 1.17, we are talking about a relatively volatile stock. Despite its resilient and conservative financial structure. Quite fascinating.

A volatile stock creates opportunities. When you get in at a reasonable valuation, there is the opportunity to - in the long run - have a great exit strategy when valuations get bloated. But we are nowhere near such a valuation, so I believe that at today's price, Dream Industrial provides great value. I show you more data to back-up this statement.

So without further ado, let me show you why I believe that the current valuation of this stock is [way] too low.

Portfolio composition and quality

As you can see, Dream Industrial is primarily focused on Canada ($4.6 billion), Followed by Europa ($2.7 billion) and the U.S. ($1 billion). The U.S. investments relate to a 29% interest in a private industrial fund.

When looking at the specific assets of the company, we can see that the portfolio is comprised out of three subcategories: Distribution Buildings, Urban Logistics and Light Industrial. The data below excludes the $1 billion in Joint Venture investments in the U.S.

I like the fact that we are talking about A-tier assets located in strategic areas (like airports, major population centers) and de-risked because of significant tenant investments in their properties (Light Industrial).

First of all, the average leasing spreads and the uptick in the occupancy (currently at 96%) shows us that Dream Industrial's portfolio is in high demand. Underscoring what managements is saying about the quality of their properties.

The leasing spreads in Ontario and Quebec really impressed me. The fact that most of the Canadian portfolio is in these two largest provinces really helps Dream Industrial in growing its FFO.

We can see that FFO growth (quarter-over-quarter) was dropping, but Net Operating Income (NOI) was accelerating. The difference can at least partly be explained by the uptick in interest rate on DREUF'S debt. Any refinancing pushes interest expenses up, therefore decreasing FFO. But the fact that NOI is climbing means that at the operational level, the portfolio is performing well. A 70% tenant retention is also good news, since it frees up more time to expand the portfolio and/or hunt for new tenants. It also means that a relatively high percentage of tenants wants to keep on to their lease, again underscoring DREUF's portfolio quality.

Leverage metrics and risk management

Since REITs use leverage to boost total returns, checking the debt ladder and debt service metric are important. We also have to take a look at the diversification efforts across tenants, to see if there is any risk of overexposure to a single tenant. Sometimes I'm not that bothered by it if an industry is quite consolidated (VICI Properties (VICI) and Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI) are great examples), but Medical Properties Trust's (MPW) exposure to its former (bankrupt) tenant, Steward, really taught the market that tenant concentration can pose a real and potentially devastating risk.

We can skip the tenant concentration risk. As you can see, the largest tenant only contributes to 2.6% of gross rental revenue. The portfolio is diversified and no single tenant represents more than 3% of gross rental revenue.

Worth mentioning is the fact that the Weighted Average Lease Term (WALT) is only 5 years for the top 10 tenants. Because of the pretty high single digit Q2 2025 leasing spreads, the relatively short leases provide a lot of room to push rents further. As we've seen, the company is doing exactly this.

When we take a look at the important debt(metrics), we can see that the debt-coverage ratios (blue line-items) are weakening. The leverage ratio has been pushed by 1.9 percentage point, meaning that the rest of the ratios is getting pushed as well. But the second (blue) line-item really jumps out, jumping from 7.0 to 8.2 in around six months. I will expand on this specific line-item because it seems worse than it is.

Net debt/EBITDA or any variant on this metric, just shows you how many years it takes to repay a company's net debt using the current operational cash flow. Assuming zero growth or additional financing. We already witnessed the large leasing spreads achieved in recent quarters. These trends from the leasing front will push the net debt/EBITDA metric lower. It is important to notice that Dream Industrial reports net debt by subtracting cash and cash equivalents. But the liquidity of a REIT also includes the credit facility. The bulk of DREUF's liquidity is in this revolver. Meaning that there is no risk of defaulting on any of the company's debt.

As management already said in their Q2 conference call, the net debt/EBITDA ratio is within their target range and as far as I can see, there is no reason to panic whatsoever. With great leasing spreads and a 60bps occupancy gain (QoQ), there is no reason to panic.

When we take a look at the debt ladder, around 28% of the company's debt is up for refinancing in between Q3 2025 - 2026. We already learned that subsequent to the quarter, a $200 million issuance of unsecured debt was done at a 4.287% rate. Interest rate swaps lowered the effective fixed rate to 3.726%, but let's just work with the 4.287%.

If we simplify the refinancing risk, we can just pretend as if the company has to refinance all of its debt at a 4.287% interest rate. At the Q2 2025 blended rate of 2.82% and taking the +/- $3 billion in debt, interest expenses will increase by $44 million per year, or $11 million every quarter. Again, this is a ridiculous scenario, but I just want to show you how safe this investment is. Especially considering that the Bank of Canada - just yesterday - cut rates by 25 basispoints and said that they are ready to cut again if necessary. A low leveraged REIT can really handle headwinds coming from an increase in interest rates.

Anyways, let's subtract $11 million from the Q2 2025 FFO number. This unrealistic impact of a complete refinancing at the current rate, will lower the Q2 2025 FFO by 14.7%. FFO per diluted unit will drop from $0.26 to $0.22. The dividend will still be covered by FFO at a payout ratio of 77%.

Calculating the dividend safety using FFO numbers is a treacherous endeavor. Many REITs (excluding many triple net REITs) have a decent amount of non-revenue-producing capital expenditures. This basically means that the funds from operations are not only used to pay a dividend, but to keep the portfolio up and running. Some of it (or a whole lot) goes to tenant incentives (TIs) needed to lease-up the portfolio. But there is also cash needed to keep the real estate in good shape. This can create a wide margin between FFO and actual free cashflow available for dividend payments. So we have to subtract all of the non-revenue-producing CapEx from the FFO.

The red line-items are important to subtract from FFO. Regarding the $19.725 million, this amount is not yet split between revenue and non-revenue- producing capital expenditures. I will only subtract the amount needed to sustain the current portfolio.

First of all, we can see that the total capital expenditures are decreasing. Most of which is attributable to four projects that were substantially completed in the past 18 months. We want to subtract non-recoverable capital expenditures, since this is the amount that is needed to keep the portfolio up-to-date.

Starting at our unrealistically low Q2 FFO of $63.834 million, we can subtract a total amount of $6.37 million ($4.3 + $2.07) to arrive at around $57.5 million. Or around $0.20 per share. We arrive at a cash available for dividends [CAD] payout ratio of 85%.

How many REITs can theoretically sustain their dividend by a [cash] 15% margin in the unrealistic event that they have to refinance all their cheap debt at once? Not many, I can tell you that.

Valuation and Risks

As you can see, the gap between FFO and CAD is not that big. Sure, it is if you include revenue-producing capital expenditures it will become wider, but that depends on your view on capital allocation. I am fine with a stagnant dividend (yes, I will address this topic in this section), if excess cash is used to grow a portfolio. In this scenario, these capital expenditures will lead to a higher future cashflow. In this scenario, dividend investors can expect a growing dividend going into the future. Because of this, I only subtract non-revenue- producing CapEx. Let us take a look at this topic.

As you can see, the dividend has been stagnant for quite a while, sure. But we entered an environment in which REITs didn't perform well. The rapid increase in the interest rates were the biggest contributing factor. But a stagnant dividend does not mean that nothing is happening from a (per share) cashflow producing perspective. As you can see, the payout ratio has been dropping by a whopping 20.3 percentage points in between 2021 - 2025. What does this mean? Well, this means that going into the future, I don't expect the dividend to remain flat. REITs, by nature, are companies that are here to pay a dividend in the first place. A 68.7% FFO payout ratio, especially with such a small gap between FFO and CAD, is low. Very low. A dividend increase makes a lot of sense right now. At least in the coming 24 months.

Excess cash has been used to repurchase shares ("units") at $10.42. Shares at the Toronto exchange currently trade at $12.38 (as of 09/17/2025). A lucrative deal. Last but not least, we can see - using a snippet from the CC - that the company is able to make accretive investments at a 1.5% spread.

There is a lot to talk about when looking at Dream Industrial, but this is just one article. So in order to conclude this chapter, I want to calculate a reasonable long-term annualized return and go over some of the risks involved in investing in this Canadian REIT.

A high double-digit total return

We can calculate the FFO growth per share from Q2 2021 - Q2 2025 using the table above.

Q2 2021: $0.19

Q2 2022: $0.22

Q2 2023: $0.25

Q2 2024: $0.25

Q2 2025: $0.26.

Looking at guidance, management expects to arrive at a 6% year-over-year growth. Since we are seeing a 4% growth in FFO using the Q2 2024 - 2025 data, I expect Q4 FFO to arrive at around $0.265. Lowering the payout ratio to around 64%. I believe that at least a normalized P/FFO of around 17x makes a lot of sense for such a conservative REIT with high bargaining power (shown by large leasing spreads) and an industry that is in demand.

I'm not saying that the revaluation of the stock will happen over night, but in a couple of years I think that a 17x P/FFO valuation is very likely. Annualizing the expected Q4 2025 FFO of $0.265, we are talking about $18 a share, + 28% at today's price.

At the current growth rate (6%) combined with the starting dividend yield (5.6%) you are buying into a monthly paying dividend stock that already provides you with a total return in the double-digits. Excluding a reasonable 28% share price appreciation in the coming years. The dividend is rock-solid and extremely safe, even in a nightmare scenario that I used to stress test the company's financials. The P/FFO multiple can easily expand when interest rates fall further, the tech bubble bursts and the dividend is getting a bump. Something that will happen anyways, since the payout ratio is steadily falling.

Risks and closing remarks

When you are investing in REITs, interest rates matter. As I have shown, I don't worry about any increase in interest expenses, since DREUF is showing great leasing spreads and FFO growth in a rising interest rate environment. If rates do climb back up, my stress test shows that this company can absorb those costs while growing its earnings per share at the same time. So the risk of climbing rates (at the back of the tariff war that Trump started) is something that I am willing to take at the current projected double-digit total return.

Worth mentioning is the fact that Dream Industrial is a Canadian REIT. Meaning that when you buy into this REIT, you are exposed to "currency risk." But as you can see, the Canadian Dollar has been losing ground (CAD:USD) against the U.S. dollar. This basically means that if you buy into Dream Industrial in the current environment, you are mitigating currency since you are buying at a relatively cheap price.

In a market dominated by bubbles and unjustifiable high valuations, REITs stand out as one of the few safe havens left. Dream Industrial gives you a predictable and growing cash flow and double-digit yearly returns for the foreseeable future. Add to this the possibility of a multiple expansion and you are getting a lot of growth at a pretty low price.

I will initiate a position this month and keep expanding as long as I can lock in a double digit yearly return that excludes any potential multiple expansion.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Quarterly Earnings: Signal Vs. Noise, Cost Vs. Benefit
data.description:
By Marc Fandetti, CFA

With the White House downplaying the value of quarterly reporting for companies, investors face a familiar question: does the cost of producing information outweigh the benefits?

Using Robert Shiller’s long-run data, this post shows that quarterly earnings contain information that is likely valuable to both long-term allocators and short-term traders. Its benefits, which I don’t attempt to quantify, should be weighed against any savings from less-frequent reporting.

Quarterly vs. Semi-Annual: What’s at Stake

The White House this week called for a change from quarterly to semi-annual earnings reporting. President Donald Trump argued that such a shift would save companies money and time.

That may be true. But would investors lose valuable information?

To answer this question, I use earnings data from Robert Shiller’s online data from January 1970 (1970:1), the year in which the Securities and Exchange Commission made quarterly earnings mandatory, to 2025:6 to test relationships among the change in three-month earnings, six-month earnings, and the trend in earnings. I define the trend as a 61-month centered moving average change in earnings. Specifically, I test whether knowing three-month earnings’ changes helps an investor better estimate changes in the longer-term trend in earnings.

Chart 1 shows three-month earnings in green, six-month earnings in red, and trend earnings in blue. Series start in January 2000 (2000:1), rather than 1970:1, for ease of visualization.

Chart 1. 3-month, 6-month, and trend earnings, 2000:1 to 2025:6.

Of course, three-month earnings are choppier than six-month earnings. But it is not obvious from visual inspection that knowing three-month earnings in addition to six-month earnings would help a long-term investor predict changes in trend earnings. (I test this below and find that they may).

It is, however, obvious that a short-term investor, one perhaps interested in earnings changes in periods of less than a year, would benefit from knowing three-month earnings. This observation is confirmed empirically below.

I start with the long-term investor, who I assume is interested in the long-term trend in earnings. A natural way to gauge the value of having three-month earnings in addition to (or instead of) six-month earnings is to model the change in trend earnings as a function of one or both, estimate that model using ordinary least squares, and compare model accuracy. In this post, I use R-squared as my measure of fit (or adjusted R-squared) — the larger, the better.

At any point, the investor knows one-half the current trend in earnings. That is, they know the first 30 months’ earnings of the current 61-month window, my proxy for the trend in earnings. And they know either the last three months of earnings, or the last six months of earnings, or both.

To determine whether receiving earnings information every three months as opposed to every six months would help the long-term investor to better predict the trend, I estimated specifications where the change in 30-month-ahead trend inflation is explained by the change in six-month earnings alone plus the prior earnings-trend change (Model 1). In Model 2, the trend change is explained by the same variables plus the three-month change in earnings. Results are shown in Table 1.

Table 1. Regressions of trend inflation change on 3- and 6-month earnings changes, 1970:1 – 2025:6.

Dependent variable = Trend inflation (30-month lead)
Model 1 Model 2
Six-mo. change (three-mo. lag) 0.073 (0.013) 0.061 (0.013)
Three-mo. change – 0.124 (0.029)
Trend change -0.223 (0.041) -0.234 (.040)
Adjusted R-squared 0.098 0.126
Obs 547 547

Source: Robert Shiller online data, author calculations.

Since I’m not interested in inference, I omit discussion of estimated coefficient values, other than to note that they enter with the expected sign. Notwithstanding this, I include the prior trend in earnings to reduce bias in my estimates, and standard errors appear in parentheses next to each estimate.

The key result is that adding quarterly earnings (three-month change) improves fit — the adjusted R-squared increases from 0.098 for Model 1 to 0.126 for Model 2. While neither fit is impressive, these results suggest that quarterly earnings may help the long-term investor predict trend earnings. Other measures of fit, namely the Akaike and Bayesian information criteria (AIC and BIC), confirm that the specification that includes 3-month earnings is more accurate.

As for what may be of interest to traders (short-term investors), one might guess that the three-month earnings change is related to the next three-month change. Quarterly earnings changes are indeed persistent. The scatter in Chart 2 shows the autocorrelation of quarterly earnings, where extreme values (earnings changes greater than 100%) have been removed for easier viewing. The estimated slope is 0.601 (se = 0.031) — the blue best-fit line is flatter than the black 45-degree diagonal line — and the R-squared is 0.361.

Chart 2. Three-month lagged earnings change vs. three-month earnings change, 1970:1 – 2025:6.

And at the risk of estimating the obvious, the R-squared of a model explaining 12-month earnings with six-month earnings (from six months before) is 0.699, whereas including three-month earnings (from three months before) improves the fit to 0.953.

Cost vs. Benefit

It is nearly axiomatic that, in most applications, more data is preferable to less. And the results discussed here suggest that quarterly earnings contain valuable information for investors. But producing earnings is costly.

As regulators consider reducing reporting frequency, they should weigh not just the savings but also the potential losses — losses to investors resulting from less transparency and to the economy resulting from impaired market efficiency.

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

Original Post

data.title: Bullish: Strong Bet On Institutional Crypto Growth
data.description:
Bullish (NYSE:BLSH) recently completed a hot IPO, making the stock difficult to value. The institutional crypto exchange owns both the Bullish Exchange and the CoinDesk brand, with major catalysts for entering the U.S. crypto exchange business and launching crypto options trading. My investment thesis is Bullish on the stock after reporting mixed Q2 results, though Bullish is definitely a very risky play.

Don't Get Lost On Weak Q2

Bullish just went public back in August, raising nearly $1.3 billion by selling 34.5 million shares at $37. The company is run by Tom Farley, a former NYSE CEO, providing a top executive to traverse the regulatory environment.

The crypto exchange reported a generally weak Q2 '25 due to lower crypto trading volumes as follows:

The company reported Q2 sales actually slumped 6% YoY to only $57 million. Bullish saw the adjusted crypto transaction revenue dip to only $24.1 million, down from $34.9 million last Q2.

The Bullish Exchange saw trading volumes dip to the $50 billion range in the months from June through August. The company started the year with $80 billion in monthly crypto volumes even with Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD) prices up during this period.

Bullish saw solid subscription revenue growing 27% to $32.9 million, but the investment story is really about the future growth in new products. The company just announced the approval of the BitLicense allowing Bullish to provide digital asset spot trading and custody services in New York state.

The company has a diverse revenue model via the Bullish Exchange and Liquidity Services along with various CoinDesk products. The business is mostly focused on crypto transaction fees and subscription services for Bullish Liquidity Services and CoinDesk Data.

The BitLicense allows Bullish to start U.S. operations on September 22. The company guided up to strong Q3 numbers based on a big boost to Subscription revenues jumping to the $45 million range, up from only $34 million in the prior quarter.

The U.S. crypto business will boost the Q4 results, especially due to the institutional focus that takes a while to launch and ramp up. In addition, Bullish is still working on launching the crypto options trading, leading to another major catalyst for the business heading into 2026.

The company focuses on institutional services and Coinbase has a report showing 83% of institutional investors plan to increase exposure to crypto. The rise of stablecoins after the U.S. government passed the GENIUS Act should drive adoption into 2026 with a platform like Bullish providing the opportunity for institutions to expand crypto exposure.

More Upside

The stock is up over 10% following the mixed Q2 results due to the strong Q3 guidance. The big question with any new IPO is gauging where the business is going in the future.

After the IPO, Bullish has a lot of excess capital. The company estimates the NAV amounts to nearly $3.6x billion with the current market cap of ~$9 billion.

The stock valuation definitely appears stretched with quarterly revenues of only $57 million. The key is the guidance and the expectations for 2026 with the launch of the U.S. crypto markets and options trading.

Bullish guided to Q3 revenues of $70+ million, so the company is getting to the annualized pace of $300 million before even adding the U.S. business. The consensus analyst estimates forecast 2026 revenues of only $307 million.

If Bullish can't vastly top these revenue targets, the stock should be avoided. The company isn't likely to add U.S. crypto operations and options trading to only generate minimal sequential growth in 2026 from running at the $300 million rate in Q3 '25.

The company already forecasts a large EBITDA profit in Q3 at $25+ million highlighting a highly profitable business model before even reaching scale. The stock would trade at about 90x the adjusted EBITDA run rate for the quarter while having a whole magnitude of catalysts, including the general adoption of crypto assets by institutions.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Bullish was a far better deal buying prior to the bump following Q2 earnings, but the stock likely has far more upside in the future. The business has multiple major growth catalysts heading into 2026 beyond just general crypto growth.

Investors should own the stock around $60, with the expectation for the company to sail past current consensus estimates. Naturally, the risk is Bullish failing to report enough growth to warrant a stock worth $8.7 billion.

data.title: CII: Strong Total Returns For Long-Term Investors And Monthly Pay
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

BlackRock Enhanced Large Cap Core Fund (NYSE:CII) is a closed-end fund that provides investors with a monthly distribution through gains in its underlying portfolio and writing options. The fund has seen its discount narrow since our last update, helping to push total returns relatively higher on a share price basis. As the discount narrows, it can make it less appealing to consider a new investment aggressively. However, the fund isn't overly expensive either and can still be a solid long-term hold.

CII Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 2.48
  • Discount/Premium: -4.02%
  • Distribution Yield: 7.61%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.93%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $954 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

CII has a simple objective and strategy. It “seeks to provide investors with a combination of current income and capital appreciation” by “investing in a portfolio of equity securities of the U.S. and foreign issuers.” They will also “employ a strategy of writing call and put options.”

As a brief reminder, and as we noted in our prior update, the fund had a change in its name and investment policy.

Since our last update, the fund has also changed its name from the BlackRock Enhanced Capital and Income Fund under the same ticker. This change took place at the start of 2025. Along with the name change, the fund's investment policy also changed.

In connection with the name change, the Board has approved the adoption of a non-fundamental investment policy to invest at least 80% of the Fund’s net assets plus the amount of any borrowings for investment purposes, in large cap equity securities and derivatives that provide investment exposure to such securities or to one or more market risk factors associated with such securities (the “80% Policy”). For purposes of the 80% Policy, large cap equity securities are equity securities that at the time of purchase have a market capitalization within the range of companies included in the Russell 1000® Index.

That said, this actually wasn't a meaningful change in the policy, but to actually help better reflect how the fund invests. This also relates to new amendments in the Investment Company Act of 1940.

After careful review of the Fund’s current investment strategies and portfolio holdings, the Fund’s investment adviser, BlackRock Advisors, LLC (“BlackRock”), recommended that the Board approve the name change and adoption of the 80% Policy in order to comply with recent amendments to Rule 35d-1 under the Investment Company Act of 1940 (often referred to as the “Names Rule”) that will go into effect in 2025 and expand the scope of the Names Rule.

Performance - Discount Narrows

The fund has provided strong total returns since our prior update, which our last focused piece was right after the tariff announcement plunge, bringing the entire equity market down. As CII is an equity fund that invests somewhat similarly to the S&P 500 Index, this fund also saw a significant decline. However, as the broader market recovered and then some to push to new all-time highs, CII also recovered.

As a call writing fund, some upside can be limited during strong runs, such as what we saw. That said, CII overwrites individual names within its portfolio, and it is around 55% overwritten. That means a rather significant portion of the portfolio will not be limited to an upside cap. That can help during these sharp market recoveries compared to a fund that overwrites 100% of its portfolio.

Driving the bulk of those total return results was the recovery in the underlying portfolio of the fund. However, some of it also came from the fact that the fund saw its discount narrow a touch during this time—going from closer to a 7% discount to only around 4% today. That would have helped contribute to the fund's total share price return a bit more during this period. It is not trading at a slightly narrower discount compared to its longer-term history.

The fund uses the MSCI USA Call Overwrite Index and the Russell 1000 Index as its benchmarks, and we can see that it has outperformed the overwrite index in all time frames. On the other hand, it has struggled to keep up with the straight Russell 1000 Index. That goes back to the overwrite portion of the portfolio, with covered calls limiting some upside during strong market periods.

We've been pretty spoiled overall with strong equity gains, considering the last 10 years show over 13% annualized returns, and the last three years are over 16% for the Russell 1000 Index.

That does bring up the other point that this fund could face headwinds as the overall equity market, primarily those indexes that represent an outsized exposure to the technology sector and, more specifically, the Magnificent 7. That also includes the Russell 1000 Index, which isn't as concentrated as the S&P 500 Index or Nasdaq Composite, but is still tilted toward those same names.

Distribution - Monthly Pay

For income-focused investors, a monthly distribution can add quite a bit of appeal relative to quarterly payouts. Of course, there are now quite a few funds in the ETF space that have started to pay weekly distributions. While that can provide even faster compounding for those reinvesting, I believe that the frequency of the distribution isn't necessarily the most important question—monthly is still going to be completely fine for most investors.

For CII, the fund's distribution rate comes to 7.61%. The NAV distribution rate looks rather reasonable at 7.28% as well, which I believe is a more important question compared to the frequency. That can help us determine whether something is likely to be more sustainable or not. In general, a higher distribution rate generally means that a fund will see NAV/share price erosion over time—a distribution rate under 8% often indicates a reasonable level where NAV erosion would be expected to be limited or not see any erosion at all.

The caveat is that this is a constantly moving metric subject to the natural gyrations and whims of the markets' overall movements. As an equity-focused fund, the distribution is largely going to be derived from capital gains for CII. Those can come from the underlying portfolio or writing options and collecting the premiums. As of their last semi-annual report, the fund reported a net investment loss of a penny. Which means that CII's payout relied entirely on capital gains.

In the six months, the options written produced a realized loss as well, which means the entire distribution would have relied on the capital gains from the underlying portfolio. During the period, there was no shortage of that either, as they realized over $92 million and paid out ~$35 million for the six months.

Selling options can generate losses when they are closed out or rolled for more than the premiums received. However, that would generally only occur when the stock rises above the strike price the managers selected. By closing out the position, the loss realized on the options can be made up for with unrealized or realized appreciation due to a higher share price.

CII's Portfolio

CII has seen a rather meaningful shift in its portfolio since our last update, that has pushed it more toward the technology sector. Previously, we saw it was around 24% of the fund, but they've raised that now to nearly 36%. Going heavier or allowing a heavier tech allocation since late February, when we last saw the updated sector allocations, is an interesting choice.

It certainly paid off, as tech was the relatively weaker sector at the start of the year and into April. Those Mag 7 names largely drove the recovery and pushed the broader equity market to new all-time highs, and that would have benefited the fund quite a bit. However, going back to what looks like historically stretched valuations for the market, I'd start to wonder if having such a high allocation to tech makes sense for now.

On the other hand, it is where most of the growth in earnings is coming from, the Mag 7 names, which means some of the higher relative valuations can be justified. The fund is also overwritten by 55%, which is outside of its 30% to 40% overwrite range "under normal market conditions." Being more overwritten would indicate that management is perhaps more bearish.

In looking at the top 10 names, we see primarily the same names we saw earlier in the year. So while the sector allocations became more concentrated, the top 10 didn't necessarily shift too much. In fact, the top 10 represent a rather concentrated 53.54%, but that isn't too much of a dramatic shift from the 50.74% allocation previously.

In total, the fund lists 32 holdings, which isn't much for an equity fund—particularly one that is listed as a diversified equity fund, but that's more related to the sector allocation being rather diverse rather than the number of holdings. Over 35% in one sector is a high allocation, but it still leaves plenty of exposure elsewhere for it to be considered diversified.

With that said, CII is looking more like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) in terms of its sector allocation these days, as SPY carries around 35% in technology as well. For comparison, the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), an ETF representation of CII's listed benchmark, has a technology sector weighting of 31.72%. So it too is looking quite tech-heavy these days.

Conclusion

We are looking pretty stretched in valuations relative to historical levels. During April, the market pulled back and created a buying opportunity, but since then, we've rallied right back to overvalued levels. That's also besides CII's own discount, which looks rather shallow these days. I believe a long-term investor can continue to "Hold" here or even accumulate in smaller chunks; however, before buying more aggressively, I'd personally wait for an overall market correction. That could result in CII's discount widening back out during the volatility and create an even better entry price.
